danger_signals.meaning: Immediate loss of life; character reverts to default weapon

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy bullets/missiles on screen moving toward player
Countdown timer display at top of screen in 3D stages
danger_signals.meaning: Player loses life if base stage not completed in time

danger_signals.visual_form: Screen edge or void at bottom of scrolling level
Enemy soldiers in red uniforms (distinct from other enemy types)
danger_signals.meaning: Dangerous combatants; carry weapon upgrades but deal lethal damage

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual electrical effect blocking passageways in base stages
danger_signals.meaning: Lethal obstacle; generator must be destroyed to proceed

safety_signals.visual_form: Flying item capsules with Falcon-shaped symbols (marked F, S, R, B, M, L)
safety_signals.meaning: Upgrade availability; increases combat effectiveness and survivability

safety_signals.visual_form: Falcon-shaped symbol marked 'B' appearing in certain areas
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability; protection from lethal contact
Increased firing speed; faster enemy elimination

safety_signals.visual_form: Terrain surfaces visible in jungle and base stages
safety_signals.meaning: Safe traversal; allows movement and avoids bottomless pits

safety_signals.visual_form: Reaching stage exit or destroying final boss/objective
safety_signals.meaning: Progression to next area; advancement toward mission success

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Falcon symbol (repurposed as weapon upgrade markers in later versions)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Red-clad guards (infantry), Galma Kilma (stage 1 boss), Godomuga (stage 2 boss), alien/organic creatures (later stages)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military bases with technological infrastructure; jungle infiltration points; alien lair aesthetic in later stages

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue (Bill's bandana/tank top color) and Red (Lance's bandana)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Two player character variants: Bill (blond, white tank top, blue bandana) vs. Lance (dark-haired, shirtless, red bandana)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Military bases under construction/control; infiltration approach vectors; point-based mission progression (Point A, B, C)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Jungle terrain transitioning to military base exterior; visible fortress architecture with technological elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy patrols and defensive emplacements; base entrance sensors as environmental hazards; sequential point-based objectives (Point A → Point B → Point C)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Interior base corridors with electrical barriers and generators; 3D pseudo-perspective showing depth and passageways; time-limit UI indicating defensive systems active
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased enemy density; electrical obstacles requiring destruction of generators; escalating difficulty and enemy behavior complexity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alien lair aesthetic; giant eyeball boss; core area with exposed weak points; otherworldly environment distinct from military bases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss attack patterns with fireball projectiles; player must destroy defense system before exposing critical target; climactic final battle scenario

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Transition sequence showing character entering enemy stronghold; cutscene (Famicom version) or text/title card (NES version) indicating communication becoming impossible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Narrative checkpoint; mission becomes all-or-nothing; no retreat possible

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Island explosion animation; helicopter extraction sequence (Famicom features extended version); flag counter increasing with replays (max 6 flags Famicom version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Staff roll sequences; secret message appears if Start + Select held during explosion (Famicom); game returns to map start for replay

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Red Falcon remains threat (post-victory secret message)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Hidden text overlay during explosion animation (Famicom version only)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Narrative twist revealing antagonist survival and continued threat; implies sequel/ongoing conflict

environmental_storytelling.technique: Architectural progression from natural to technological
environmental_storytelling.example: Jungle stage → military base exterior → base interior corridors → alien lair core; visual escalation communicates invasion depth and enemy fortress sophistication
Countdown timer in 3D base stages shows base defense systems actively responding; time pressure narrativizes urgency of destroying generators
Electric barriers blocking passageways require generator destruction; visual logic reinforces infiltration progression and base vulnerability
Falcon-shaped symbols containing weapons appear in contextual locations (destroyed pill boxes, defeated guards); distribution reinforces combat readiness narrative

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss arena design as story beat
environmental_storytelling.example: Stage bosses (Galma Kilma, Godomuga, final eyeball) presented in distinctive visual spaces; each represents faction stronghold or defense system

environmental_storytelling.technique: Famicom map screen as world communication
environmental_storytelling.example: Side-view archipelago map with top-down position indicator shows Galuga Archipelago geography; visually establishes mission scope and player location relative to overall strategy
Stage-between cutscenes show character radio communications ('Point A destroyed', 'Proceeding to Point B'); text + sprite animation narrate mission progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Default facing direction; weapon held at ready; can aim in multiple directions (left, right, up)
Can shoot horizontally and diagonally while in motion; sprite shows directional momentum
Curls into somersault animation; can shoot in 8 directions including downward; peak height and landing location indicate threat avoidance
Prone/stomach position; shoots low targets; avoids high-trajectory enemy fire; visually communicates defensive posture
Reverts to default rifle weapon; loses equipped power-up; sprite knockback indicates damage direction
Identical movement/combat behavior to Bill; visual distinction (red bandana, dark hair, shirtless) marks player 2; indicates multiplayer role separation
Shoots at player; drops weapon upgrades when defeated; indicates high threat level and loot value
Specific attack pattern; indicates stage 1 completion requirement; visual distinctiveness marks boss-tier enemy
Arm segments spawn and extend sequentially in linked pattern; visual feedback shows structural vulnerability and attack sequence
Visible but shielded by defense system; indicates two-stage defeat mechanism
Shoots fireballs in attack pattern; indicates vulnerability window and final confrontation climax
Visible bullet/missile sprite moving across screen; indicates active threat requiring avoidance or destruction
Impassable visual effect; blocks forward progress; indicates generator destruction requirement

notes: Contra (NES) employs a two-tier visual communication system: mechanical/physical (weapons, platforms, enemies) and narrative/environmental (base progression, time limits, stage transitions). The Famicom version adds substantial cutscene narrative and animated backgrounds not present in NES versions, indicating regional differences in story emphasis. Weapon upgrade system uses Falcon-shaped visual markers that ironically repurpose antagonist faction symbolism as collectible indicators. The game's world-state communication is primarily driven by stage architecture (jungle → exterior base → interior corridors → alien core) and time-based UI elements that narrativize urgency. Danger signals are predominantly color-based (red guards), collision-based (projectiles, enemies), and environmental (pits, electrical barriers). The 3D perspective stages use top-down map displays to orient players within base structure, creating spatial storytelling. Two-player character differentiation (Bill vs. Lance) is purely visual with no mechanical difference, serving faction cohesion visualization.
